So, Ursula M. Franklin is a Canadian scientist who, in 1989,
gave a series of lectures titled The Real World of Technology.
In it, she talks about how technology is not just the technological artifact that we put
out into the world, but also how it affects the world, which in turn affects new technologies.
This is kind of like the feedback loop that Rudy from Black Sky was talking about yesterday
in his talk.
So, Ursula says, "As I see it, technology has built the house in which we all live.
The house is continually being extended and remodeled.
More and more of human life takes place within its walls, so that today there is hardly any human
activity that does not occur within this house.
We all are affected by the design of the house, the division of its space, and the location
of its doors and walls."
That was written in 1989, but I think it's clear where I'm going with this metaphor.
The internet is the house we live in.
We are all affected by the, hopefully it's playing, the division of its space, the location

And I think that's why the artist Spencer Chang's work resonates with a lot of people because
it makes the movement through this internet house visible when it is so often invisible

So if that is the case, if the internet is now the house we all live in, then what is
the role of technologists?
The way I see it, we build not only the house, but we also dictate how the house gets built,
and by whom, through things like standards and protocols.

Okay, but back to the title of the talk.
In ecology, rewilding is a process where humans do an initial intervention in a given place or land in order to restore the ecosystem.
And the hope is that these seeds of intervention will seed natural processes and increase biodiversity.
So I think in a lot of ways that our work as developers in and of this protocol is to make these little seeds of intervention on the web
in order to enable others to use and build on the web as a sort of natural and organic process on its own.
So I care about all of these things, but I really care about the second point that builders on a protocol and on the web for social experiences shouldn't just be software engineers and companies.
This idea of biodiversity or diversity in general comes up not just in ecology but across disciplines.
In the dawn of everything, David Graber and David Wengrau reconsider the narrative of human history.
So this history is usually discussed as a straight line from hunter-gatherer society to agricultural revolution to industrial revolution to now.
The Davids challenge this notion and point out how during the long time where humans were thought to be hunter-gatherers,
there's not too much evidence of what they were doing, so it's easy to spin a bunch of narratives on top of that.
But if you do look at the evidence, there seems to be a plurality of societal forms.
So there are hunter-gatherer societies, but also nomadic farmers or chiefdoms or different combinations of this depending on seasonality.
So they say, maybe the real question is, how did we get stuck?
How did we end up in one single mode?
Wendell Berry is another one of my favorite writers and a lifelong farmer.
And he talks about the importance of diversity to farming, both at a local level where a diversity of crops is needed to sustain soil,
but also at a global level where each farm being in harmony with the land lends to a diversity of ways of doing farming,
as opposed to a cash crop or industrialized farming that treats all land as the same and therefore depletes it.
And in this essay, Maria and Robin, who I think is here today, write that ecologists know that diversity is resilient.
I think this concept goes beyond ecology.
I think this is true of the internet as well.
An internet where one data center going down means the web goes down is not resilient,
and where one platform insidifying means everyone's experiences are insidified is not resilient.

So I think the key for app protocol to survive as a protocol and ecosystem is for us to be resilient by supporting the necessary diversity of infrastructures, technologies, social networks, and communities that human life demands.
Right now, this lack of diversity online is a really strongly felt experience.
In the physical world, your library doesn't look or feel like your kitchen, your bedroom, your school, your patio, etc.
But online, this is absolutely the case.
My hope is that with app protocol, we can shape our digital habitats with as much agency as we can our physical ones.
So in the real world of technology, Ursula M. Franklin talks about how hard it is to define technology as a concept.
However, one definition we might be able to agree upon is technology as a way of doing something.
So, if the current state of the web is just one way of doing the social internet, then let's build the social internet differently.
Thank you.
Let's give a round of applause.
